description: The provided image is unreadable and appears as a system placeholder icon, preventing direct visual verification of subpanels, axes, or legends. Based on the comprehensive paper context for the low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al, density ~7.40 g/cm3), this Figure likely presents a central dataset elucidating the anisotropic deformation behavior between rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD) specimens processed by homogenization at 1100°C, hot rolling, cold rolling, and intercritical annealing at 850°C for 10 min. The microstructure comprises banded delta-ferrite (~44 vol.%, ~38 um) and ultrafine-grained gamma+alpha aggregates (~31 vol.% austenite ~1.8 um; ~25 vol.% alpha-ferrite ~2 um). Key trends reported include superior tensile properties in RD (UTS ~864 MPa, elongation ~69%, strain hardening rate ~2052 MPa) versus TD (~1828 MPa), attributed to direction-dependent strain partitioning and TRIP kinetics. In situ HEXRD and micro-DIC indicate that RD deformation approaches an iso-strain condition between delta-ferrite and gamma+alpha aggregates, whereas TD exhibits pronounced strain heterogeneity with higher delta-ferrite strain (~1.26x) and slower martensite transformation (~4 vol.% lower at true strain ~0.48). These observations support the conclusion that anisotropic TRIP behavior and strain partitioning govern the directional mechanical response.

Figure 1 displays a pseudo-binary equilibrium phase diagram for the Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al (wt.%) steel system calculated by Thermo-Calc with the TCFE9 database. Temperature (500–1600°C) is plotted against aluminum content (0–10 wt.%). Visible phase fields include liquid (L), γ+L, α+L, single-phase austenite (γ), γ+α, γ+θ, γ+α+κ, and α+κ, where α denotes ferrite stabilized by aluminum. A red star labeled "Current Study" marks the selected composition of approximately 5 wt.% Al and the intercritical annealing temperature of 850°C. The caption states that aluminum addition promotes δ-ferrite formation and lowers density by 6.2%. The chosen annealing point lies within the γ+α two-phase region, guiding the processing route—homogenization at 1100°C, hot rolling above 1000°C, cold rolling, and intercritical annealing at 850°C for 10 min followed by water quenching—to obtain the desired ultrafine γ+α aggregate plus ferrite microstructure.

Figure 2 presents the in-situ synchrotron high-energy X-ray diffraction (HEXRD) setup and analysis pipeline for a low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium manganese steel. Panel (a) schematically illustrates the experimental geometry using fixed 60 keV photons, a beam stop, a slit system, and a Varex area detector that records Debye-Scherrer rings as a function of the azimuthal angle η and the scattering vector Q, with dog-bone specimens loaded along either the rolling direction (RD) or the transverse direction (TD). Panel (b) shows two-dimensional diffraction intensity maps plotted against azimuth angle (0–360°) and 2-Theta for the non-deformed state and after ~30% applied strain in RD and TD, displaying distinct Debye-Scherrer rings attributable to FCC austenite (γ) and BCC ferrite (α/α′) phases. Panel (c) gives the corresponding integrated 1D diffraction profiles (experimental open symbols and Rietveld-calculated solid lines), clearly resolving five FCC reflections ({111}, {200}, {220}, {311}, {222}) and four BCC reflections ({110}, {200}, {211}, {220}), with excellent agreement between experiment and calculation. Panel (d) shows the sector-integrated profile (azimuth −5° to +5° along the tensile axis) deconvoluted by single-peak Gaussian fitting into individual FCC (green) and BCC (blue) peaks, enabling precise determination of interplanar spacing for lattice strain analysis. Collectively, these data provide the experimental foundation for quantifying phase fractions, lattice strains, and deformation-induced martensitic transformation under anisotropic loading conditions.

Figure 3 presents comprehensive EBSD characterization of the initial non-deformed microstructure in a low-density medium-Mn steel processed by hot rolling, cold rolling, and intercritical annealing at 850 °C for 10 min. Subfigures (a) and (c) show 3D reconstructions of Phase + IQ and IPF mappings, respectively, assembled from three orthogonal planes (RD, TD, ND), revealing a distinct banded or layered structure aligned along the rolling direction. The phase map in (a) indicates 61.2 % BCC and 38.8 % FCC. Subfigure (b) provides a magnified RD-TD Phase + IQ view resolving the ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ-austenite and α-ferrite aggregate alongside coarse δ-ferrite grains; the uniform BCC contrast confirms the absence of martensite in the as-annealed condition. Subfigures (d) and (e) quantify the bimodal grain size distribution: austenite averages ~1.8 μm, α-ferrite ~2 μm, and δ-ferrite ~38 μm. This stark size contrast enables differentiation of the two BCC phases, establishing a heterogeneous starting microstructure that underpins the subsequent anisotropic deformation behavior.

This figure illustrates the direction-dependent tensile response of an intercritically annealed low-density medium-Mn steel (Fe–7.95Mn–0.25C–4.94Al, 850 °C/10 min WQ) along the rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD). Subfigure (a) presents the engineering stress–strain curves, where the RD specimen reaches an ultimate tensile strength of ~864 MPa and a total elongation of ~69%, both exceeding the TD specimen values. Subfigure (b) plots the strain hardening rate (upper curves) and true stress–strain curves (lower curves) versus true strain. The hardening response is classified into three stages: an initial rapid drop (Stage I), a rise beginning at ~0.2 true strain (Stage II), and a final decrease until necking governed by the Considère criterion (Stage III). The RD sample registers a higher peak strain hardening rate (2052 MPa) compared with the TD sample (1828 MPa). These curves collectively establish that the RD orientation delivers a superior strength–ductility synergy, driven by its elevated strain hardening capacity; caption states this difference arises from faster strain-induced α′-martensite transformation kinetics in the RD sample.

This figure presents EBSD characterization of the microstructure in a low-density medium manganese steel (TD sample) after 15% engineering strain. Panel (a) shows a Phase + Band Slope (BS) map distinguishing FCC austenite (green) from BCC phases (red, including ferrite and martensite), revealing a banded microstructure of δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates. Panel (b) displays a bimodal BS distribution of the BCC phase, where the low-BS peak corresponds to deformation-induced α′-martensite (higher dislocation density/lattice distortion) and the high-BS peak to ferrite; a cutoff is applied at their intersection for phase differentiation. Panel (c) provides the corresponding kernel average misorientation (KAM) map, indicating higher local misorientation in the UFG γ+α region than in δ-ferrite due to martensitic transformation. The magnified phase map in (d) resolves γ, α/δ, and α′-martensite (gray), showing that α′-martensite nucleates primarily at austenite/ferrite phase boundaries. Panel (e) presents the KAM map of only the FCC phase, where arrows highlight elevated misorientation (and thus higher geometrically necessary dislocation density) at phase boundaries. Together, these results demonstrate that strain-induced α′-martensite preferentially nucleates at heterointerfaces owing to strain incompatibility and stress concentration, supporting the TRIP effect as a key strain-hardening mechanism.

This figure presents TEM characterization of austenite deformation mechanisms in the TD (transverse direction) sample of a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al) subjected to 15% engineering strain. Panels (a1) and (a2) show bright-field (BF) and dark-field (DF) images of a representative austenite grain, respectively, revealing strain-induced α′-martensite nucleation near the γ/α phase boundary, with corresponding selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns indexing the FCC austenite (Z=[011]) and BCC martensite/ferrite (Z=[-111]) structures. Panels (b1) and (b2) provide BF and DF images from a second austenite grain, demonstrating analogous α′-martensite formation adjacent to the γ/δ phase boundary, accompanied by matching diffraction patterns. The DF images confirm the crystallographic identity of the α′-martensite laths. Together, these micrographs provide direct evidence that strain-induced martensitic transformation preferentially initiates at austenite/ferrite heterophase interfaces, supporting the mechanism that strain incompatibility and stress concentration at these boundaries drive the γ→α′ transformation in this lightweight medium-Mn steel.

This figure presents TEM characterization of the deformation substructures in a low-density medium manganese steel (TD sample, 15% engineering strain). Panels (a1) and (a2) show bright-field (BF) and high-resolution TEM images of an austenite (γ) grain with FCC structure (zone axis [011]), revealing dense stacking faults (SFs) via planar dislocation slip, as marked by red arrows in the magnified inset of (a1) and atomically resolved in (a2). Panels (b1) and (b2) depict a δ-ferrite grain with BCC structure (zone axis [-111]), showing a high-density dislocation wall in (b1) and dislocation tangles under two-beam condition (g=110) in (b2). Panels (c1) and (c2) display an α-ferrite grain (BCC, zone axis [-111]), also exhibiting dislocation tangles under two-beam condition (g=110). The contrasting deformation modes—planar slip with SFs in austenite versus dislocation cross-slip forming tangles in both ferrite phases—are attributed to the lower stacking fault energy (SFE) of austenite compared to ferrite, supporting the anisotropic strain partitioning and phase-specific deformation mechanisms.

Figure 8 presents in-situ synchrotron HEXRD results and quantitative phase analysis revealing the anisotropic strain-induced martensitic transformation in a low-density medium-Mn steel. Panel (a) displays diffraction patterns at selected true strains (ε = 0.14, 0.22, 0.3, and 0.38) for samples loaded along the rolling direction (RD, red curves) and transverse direction (TD, blue curves). The FCC austenite peaks (γ311 and γ222) progressively diminish with increasing strain, while the BCC/BCT martensite/ferrite peaks (α211 and α220) intensify; notably, the BCC/BCT peaks in the RD sample are consistently higher than those in the TD sample at equivalent strains, particularly above ε ≈ 0.22. Panel (b) plots the deformation-induced martensite volume fraction determined by the Rietveld refinement method as a function of true strain: the RD sample exhibits faster transformation kinetics, reaching approximately 25% martensite at ε ≈ 0.5 compared to roughly 21% for the TD sample, with a divergence of about 4 vol.% near ε ≈ 0.48. The inset shows corresponding strain hardening rate curves, where RD maintains a higher hardening rate than TD at elevated strains. These observations demonstrate that the higher strain-hardening ability of the RD sample stems from accelerated strain-induced α′-martensite transformation kinetics.

Figure 9 presents the dislocation density analysis of austenite in a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt%) deformed along the rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD). Panel (a) shows modified Williamson–Hall plots of ΔK–βW_hkl versus KC^1/2 for five FCC crystallographic reflections ({111}, {200}, {220}, {311}, {222}) at true strains of ~0.14 (β_RD=0.002, β_TD=0.004) and ~0.22 (β_RD=0.0018, β_TD=0.0044). The linear fits for RD exhibit consistently steeper slopes than those for TD, indicating a higher dislocation density in the RD sample. Panel (b) plots austenite dislocation density as a function of true strain; RD maintains higher values throughout deformation (maximum difference ~1.51×10^14 m^-2), but the gap narrows above true strain 0.35. This anisotropy is attributed to strain-induced α′-martensitic transformation and associated strain partitioning, while the convergence at high strain reflects the eventual martensitic transformation of highly dislocated austenite, contributing to the decay in strain hardening.

Figure 10 illustrates the strain partitioning behavior in the rolling direction (RD) sample of a low-density medium manganese steel. Panels (a1) and (b1) present EBSD phase and band contrast maps showing the entire probed area (100 μm scale) and a magnified ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α region (5 μm scale, red frame in a1), respectively, revealing banded coarse δ-ferrite adjacent to the UFG aggregate. Panels (c1) and (d1) provide single-phase maps of the UFG region for the FCC (austenite) and BCC (ferrite) phases, respectively, with a legend indicating blue for FCC and gray for BCC. Panels (a2–a5), (b2–b5), (c2–c5), and (d2–d5) display the corresponding shear strain distributions at engineering strains of 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. As strain increases, the maps progress from relatively uniform low-strain (blue) to heterogeneous high-strain (yellow/red) patterns; at 20%, red arrows in (a5) and black arrows in (d5) highlight intense strain localization. Graphs (e1) and (e2) plot local shear strain versus distance across representative δ and γ+α regions, showing comparable strain levels (~14–18%) across the phase boundaries in the RD sample, which supports an iso-strain-like partitioning condition between δ-ferrite and the UFG γ+α aggregate and helps explain the anisotropic deformation behavior.

This figure presents the strain partitioning behavior in the transverse direction (TD) sample of a low-density medium manganese steel investigated via EBSD phase mapping coupled with microscopic digital image correlation (μ-DIC). Panel (a1) displays an EBSD phase plus band contrast map of the full probed area (100 μm scale bar), revealing a banded architecture of coarse δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates. Panel (b1) provides a magnified view of the UFG γ+α region indicated by the red rectangle in (a1), with corresponding single-phase maps showing the FCC austenite (c1) and BCC ferrite/martensite (d1) distributions at 5 μm scale. Panels (a2-a5), (b2-b5), (c2-c5), and (d2-d5) present the evolution of shear strain distribution from 5% to 20% engineering strain for the regions in (a1), (b1), (c1), and (d1), respectively, using a color scale from blue (low strain) to red/yellow (high strain). These maps demonstrate that deformation induces pronounced strain heterogeneity in the TD orientation, with the soft δ-ferrite channels accommodating substantially higher shear strain than the surrounding UFG matrix. Within the UFG region, strain preferentially accumulates in the FCC phase relative to the BCC phase. Panel (e) quantifies this partitioning through local shear strain line profiles across phase boundaries, showing δ-ferrite peaks near 30% shear strain whereas adjacent γ+α regions remain near 5-15%, supporting an iso-stress-like deformation response in the TD sample that contrasts with the more homogeneous iso-strain behavior observed in the rolling direction sample.

Figure 12 presents four line graphs quantifying the average shear strain as a function of engineering strain in a low-density medium Mn steel, comparing rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD) samples. The first two panels show the strain partitioning between δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates: in RD, both phases accumulate similar shear strain linearly from ~3.5% to ~15%, indicating an iso-strain-like condition, whereas in TD, the δ-ferrite strain is markedly higher than that of the γ+α aggregates (by a factor of ~1.26), revealing strain concentration in the softer δ-ferrite. The latter two panels resolve the strain distribution within the UFG γ+α region, comparing the γ-α' mixed phase against α-ferrite: for both directions, the γ-α' mixed phase bears higher shear strain than the α phase (up to ~1.3 times), increasing linearly with applied strain. The lower strain concentrated in the austenite phase for the TD sample relative to RD explains the reduced kinetics of strain-induced martensitic transformation and lower strain-hardening capability in the TD orientation.

Figure 13 presents in-situ high-energy X-ray diffraction (HEXRD) measurements of lattice strain evolution in a low-density Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al medium-manganese steel deformed along the rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD). Panels (a) and (b) display the lattice strain of individual crystallographic reflections—FCC (111), (200), (220), (311), (222) for austenite and BCC (110), (200), (211), (220) for ferrite—as a function of applied engineering strain; the differing initial slopes reflect elastic anisotropy. Panels (c) and (d) show the weighted average lattice strains for the FCC and BCC phases, with arrows marking the onset of plasticity. Austenite deviates from linearity earlier, at 1.8% strain in RD and 2.1% in TD, whereas ferrite yields later, at 2.3% in RD and 2.9% in TD. After macroscopic yielding, the austenite lattice strain slightly decreases, indicating stress relaxation, while ferrite strain continues to rise. These results confirm that austenite is the softer phase that yields first and reveal anisotropic, direction-dependent strain partitioning.

Figure 14 presents the deformation-induced martensitic transformation kinetics as a function of true strain for a low-density medium-Mn steel processed by hot rolling, cold rolling, and intercritical annealing at 850°C for 10 min. The plot compares experimental martensite fractions determined by in-situ HEXRD during tensile testing along the rolling direction (Exp.RD, red open circles) and the transverse direction (Exp.TD, blue open circles) against predictions from the Olson-Cohen (OC) model (black dashed lines). The RD sample exhibits consistently faster transformation kinetics than the TD sample, reaching approximately 25% martensite fraction at a true strain of ~0.48 compared to ~20-21% for the TD sample. The OC model, using parameters fitted to the TD data and accounting for a higher austenite plastic strain in RD (caption reports ε_RD = 1.15 ε_TD based on micro-DIC data), closely reproduces the experimental trends for both directions. This comparison supports the conclusion that loading-direction-dependent strain partitioning governs the anisotropic kinetics of strain-induced martensitic transformation in this steel.

This figure presents microscopic digital image correlation (μ-DIC) maps showing the local shear-strain distribution near the phase boundary between δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates in the rolling-direction (RD) sample of a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%). Panels (a) and (b) correspond to two representative regions, each containing four sub-images captured at engineering tensile strains of 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. The color scale indicates shear strain from 0% to 30%. Dashed black lines mark the δ-ferrite/UFG γ+α phase boundaries, and red arrows highlight localized strain concentration sites. At 5% strain both phases exhibit low shear strain (blue), whereas at 15% and 20% pronounced strain localization (yellow to red) develops along the phase boundaries and within the UFG aggregate region. The scale bar represents 4 μm. These μ-DIC data support the caption and text statements that the δ-ferrite and UFG γ+α aggregates in the RD sample deform under an iso-strain-like condition, yet strain incompatibility at the phase boundaries generates significant strain concentrations. This localized strain promotes geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation and increases the local driving force for strain-induced martensite nucleation, contributing to the higher martensite transformation kinetics observed in the RD sample.
